---
title: "Prediction Background: WTI Crude Oil Close Below \\$100 on June 26, 2026"
---

# The Prediction Question

**Will the front-month WTI crude oil futures contract (Yahoo ticker `CL=F`) close below \$100.00 per barrel on June 26, 2026?**

*Resolution:* YES if the regular-session settlement price of the active CL=F front-month contract on Friday, June 26, 2026 is **strictly less than \$100.00 per barrel**. NO otherwise (including exactly \$100.00). On June 26, 2026 the front-month is the **August 2026 contract (CLQ26)** — the July 2026 contract (CLN26) expires June 22, 2026. The settlement source is CME Group / NYMEX.

---

# Current Market State (as of May 26, 2026)

| Series | Approximate price |
|---|---|
| WTI front-month (CL=F) | **~\$92–96 / bbl** |
| Brent front-month | ~\$103.50 / bbl |
| WTI–Brent spread | ~\$7 (slightly wider than usual) |
| WTI 1-month at-the-money implied volatility | **~86 % annualised** |
| Recent low | < \$90 (first sub-\$90 print in ~3 weeks) |

WTI dropped more than 10 % over the past week on rising optimism around a US–Iran interim deal. The market is highly headline-sensitive — daily moves of \$2–4 have been common in May.

---

# Key Geopolitical Backdrop

- **Middle East crisis since Feb 28, 2026.** US- and Israeli-led strikes against Iran triggered a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly **20 %** of global seaborne oil and LNG normally transits.
- **Production shut-ins.** Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the UAE, Qatar and Bahrain have collectively idled **10–11 million barrels per day** of crude for as long as the strait remains effectively closed.
- **US–Iran negotiations.** Talks toward an interim ceasefire deal that would reopen the strait are "proceeding nicely" according to recent US statements, but key issues — Iran's enriched-uranium stockpile, transit tolls on Hormuz — remain unresolved.
- **EIA assumption.** The May 2026 *Short-Term Energy Outlook* assumes the strait remains effectively closed through late May, with shipping traffic gradually picking up in June. Pre-conflict throughput is not expected before late 2026.

---

# Institutional Forecasts

| Source (publication date) | Brent Q2 2026 | Brent Q3 2026 | Implied WTI Q2 (≈ Brent – \$5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EIA STEO (May 2026)** | **\$109.73** | \$99.09 | ~\$104–105 |
| EIA STEO 2026 annual avg | \$94.85 | — | ~\$90 |
| **J.P. Morgan Global Research (2026)** | range \$95–115 | range \$80–100 | ~\$90–110 |
| Implied by current futures curve | ~\$95 (WTI Jul) | ~\$92 (WTI Sep) | spot ~\$92 |

There is an unusually wide gap between institutional forecasts (which still expect prices to rebound toward and above \$100 in Q2 on Hormuz disruption) and the **futures market**, which is currently pricing the front month near \$92 — i.e., expecting a deal/normalisation outcome.

---

# Calendar of Catalysts Before June 26, 2026

| Date | Event | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Jun 7, 2026** | OPEC+ ministerial meeting | Decision on whether to extend/deepen voluntary cuts or continue unwinding ~1 mb/d. A surprise cut → bullish; further unwinding → bearish. |
| **Jun 10, 2026 (approx)** | EIA June STEO | Updated price forecast; market often re-prices on STEO releases. |
| **Jun 11–22, 2026** | Window for plausible US–Iran interim deal announcement | A deal that reopens Hormuz could trigger a \$10–20 down-move; talks collapsing could trigger an equally large up-move. |
| **Jun 22, 2026** | CLN26 (July contract) expiry → CL=F rolls to CLQ26 (August contract) | Front-month roll typically causes a small price discontinuity reflecting the term-structure slope. |
| **Jun 26, 2026** | **Resolution date** | Friday close. |

---

# Volatility-Based Sanity Check

With WTI 1-month at-the-money implied vol around 86 %, the implied one-day log-return σ is roughly $86\% / \sqrt{252} \approx 5.4\%$, and the one-month σ is roughly $86\% / \sqrt{12} \approx 25\%$.

Taking a current price near \$93, a one-month one-σ band is roughly \$72 to \$120 (geometric). The \$100 threshold sits roughly **+0.3 σ** above current — easily reachable in either direction. Under a no-drift lognormal model, the implied probability of being below \$100 at horizon is around **0.55–0.65**, depending on the exact spot price assumed.

---

# Historical Reference Points

| Period | WTI peak | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Mar–Jun 2022 | \$123 | Russia–Ukraine invasion shock |
| Jun 2008 | \$147 | Pre-GFC commodity super-cycle |
| Apr 2020 | –\$37 (briefly) | COVID demand collapse + WTI Cushing storage saturation |
| Feb–May 2026 | \$118 (intraday) | Hormuz closure |

The **2022 analogue** is instructive: a comparable supply-shock rally took prices well above \$100 and held them there for ~4 months before mean reversion brought WTI back into the \$80s by year-end. The current 2026 episode has so far peaked higher (in nominal terms) and fallen faster on diplomatic developments.

---

# What Pushes Probability of YES (close < \$100) Up

- Front month is already trading **below \$100** with a downward bias.
- US–Iran negotiations look serious; any concrete announcement before June 26 would likely drive a sharp drop.
- The futures curve is in mild contango — the market is pricing further softening into late summer.
- OPEC+ has been **unwinding** cuts in 2026; further unwinding at the June 7 meeting would be bearish.
- High inventories built during the shut-in period would flood back into the market on Hormuz reopening, amplifying any sell-off.

# What Pushes Probability of YES Down

- Institutional forecasts (EIA, JPM) still see Q2 2026 prices well above \$100 in their central scenarios.
- A **breakdown** in US–Iran talks — or a renewed escalation — could spike prices by \$10–20 in a single session.
- 10–11 mb/d of shut-in supply remains an existential overhang that has not yet been resolved.
- An OPEC+ surprise cut on June 7 would be bullish.
- One-month implied volatility of 86 % implies wide outcome distributions in both directions — the \$8 cushion below \$100 is only ~0.3 σ.
- The futures market has been wrong before; betting against EIA when it forecasts \$10 above spot has historically been profitable about half the time.

---

# Sources

- [EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (May 2026)](https://www.eia.gov/outlooks/steo/)
- [Oil prices post weekly loss as US and Iran signal progress toward a deal — CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/22/oil-prices-today-trump-iran-strait-of-hormuz-uranium-.html)
- [Crude Oil drops as US inches toward Iran deal — Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-24/latest-oil-market-news-and-analysis-for-may-25)
- [Oil prices fall amid mixed signals on US–Iran peace deal — Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2026/5/25/oil-prices-fall-amid-mixed-signals-on-us-iran-peace-deal)
- [J.P. Morgan Global Research: Oil Price Forecast 2026](https://www.jpmorgan.com/insights/global-research/commodities/oil-prices)
- [Crude Oil WTI Jun '26 Futures Options — Barchart](https://www.barchart.com/futures/quotes/CLM26/options)
- [Crude Oil Futures Quotes — CME Group](https://www.cmegroup.com/markets/energy/crude-oil/light-sweet-crude.quotes.html)
- [IEA Oil Market Report — May 2026](https://www.iea.org/reports/oil-market-report-may-2026)
- [Polymarket: WTI price in May 2026](https://polymarket.com/event/what-price-will-wti-hit-in-may-2026)
